focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 16

By morning the spray had dried tacky on the rails, a sweet chemical film that held whatever touched it: spoonbill feather dust, mosquito wings, gray ash from the generator stack, the soft down that had come off the dead chicks when Beto lifted them by the handful into lined fish boxes. Mina scrubbed her hands with the camp's orange mechanic soap until the skin in her palms looked peeled, but when she lifted them to her face she still got diesel, mangrove rot, and that pesticide sweetness underneath, as if the smell had moved into the small cracks around her nails during the night and found lease terms.

She had slept in pieces. Every time the stilts shifted under wind she had heard the impact again, not the spray run itself but the debris after it: wet little bodies on walkway planks, the slap of fish turning white-side up in the channel, Beto saying I got this one and then not sounding nineteen at all. Just before dawn she had stood by the sink in the dark and watched a spoonbill feather stuck to the drain pulse with the last of the rinse water. It had been almost beautiful, which made her want to break something.

Imogen sent for her at eight.

The main house office still carried last night's triage in a dozen petty ways. A stack of wet incident forms bowed on the desk where somebody had set them down with bloody gloves. The battery lantern on the file cabinet had burned a pale ring into the wood. One of the donor welcome baskets had been shoved under a chair and forgotten there, local citrus rolling loose among the brochures. Imogen stood at the open jalousie window in a field shirt she had buttoned wrong in her hurry, silver hair pinned up with the red golf pencil she used for guest counts. A smear of pink guano crossed one cuff. It looked less like fi...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ehead against Mina's.

"When the facts start costing you," she said softly, "don't rearrange them to make yourself bearable."

Mina's taped fingers tightened at the nape of Rhea's neck. She saw Tomas on the porch with the ledger, the annex badge facedown on her bunk, every version she had ever offered with the blood edited out.

Mina put her good hand on the back of Rhea's neck. "I won't," she said. "If I start, stop me."

The laptop chimed. The upload was complete.

Rhea kissed her once, brief, chicory-bitter, and without apology. Then she stepped back, capped the thermos, and unhooked the county shirt from where it had caught on the corner of the recorder case.

"Sleep with ointment on those hands," she said.

"Yes, doctor."

"Not a doctor."

"You say that every time."

"Because it keeps being true."

Mina smiled despite herself. Rhea picked up the dry bag and headed for the door. At the threshold she looked back, braid dark down her shoulder, one hand already lifting to scratch at the mosquito bite on her collarbone now that nobody else was close enough to see her break discipline.

"Tomorrow," she said, "if Imogen asks, come get me first."

"I will."

Rhea nodded and was gone down the screened walk, her footfalls swallowed by the old wood almost immediately.

Mina stayed where she was until the screen door settled into its frame. On the laptop, the new file sat under its ugly string of numbers and station shorthand. A damp half-moon from Rhea's thermos had dried on the counter beside the keyboard. Mina touched the trackpad, then took her hand off it. She left the file name alone.

When she finally turned off the red light and stepped onto the walkway, the paddleboards below the bat lab still dripped creek water back into the black channel one patient drop at a time.
